title: TESO Meets Tomorrow!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. Three severe OCR intrusions were checked against the source pages and restored in the derived Tamil reading copy: `ஈழப் போரின்போது`, a clean continuation between pages 105 and 106, and `ஈழத் தமிழர்களிடையே`. The demographic statement that the Tamil share fell from 7 per cent to 48 per cent is visibly printed that way in the source and is therefore retained as the author's stated figure rather than silently altered. The canonical source files remain unchanged.
paragraphs: Although Indian Prime Minister Dr Manmohan Singh is not attending the Commonwealth meeting in Sri Lanka, External Affairs Minister Salman Khurshid has gone to participate on India's behalf. When reporters asked me about this, I replied on 13 November 2013: “My view is that it is wrong. My position is that the Union government must not disregard the feelings of the Tamil people in this manner.” Several other parties in Tamil Nadu reflected the same feeling.
Salman Khurshid has offered several reasons to justify attending the Commonwealth meeting:

1. Various matters, including the problems of Eelam Tamils, must be discussed with the Sri Lankan government. 2. It will provide an opportunity to convey India's views on granting greater powers to Eelam Tamils and on the attacks Indian fishers face when they go to sea. 3. India is engaged in various projects for the welfare of Tamils in Sri Lanka's Northern Province. 4. India is committed to ensuring that the elected government in the Northern Province succeeds politically and economically. 5. India has a duty to ensure that Tamils receive greater powers under the Constitution and the India–Sri Lanka Accord.
These are Khurshid's reasons.

Every reason offered by the Indian External Affairs Minister to justify his Sri Lankan visit has already been defeated, historically and politically, by the Sinhala majoritarian government. From the time of Thanthai Chelva to the Rajiv–Jayewardene Accord, the Sinhala government has not honoured a single agreement it entered into with the Tamil people at various stages. History shows that every one was instead violated.

Everyone knows that the Sinhala government hesitates even to treat Tamils as citizens of Sri Lanka and grant them their constitutional rights. Has there been another country where a government, with its own army, waged war upon its own citizens and caused such devastation? Is there another people living as refugees in their own land? It has happened in Eelam.

The Tamil struggle for rights has continued since Sri Lanka gained independence in 1948. Under Thanthai Chelva's leadership, a non-violent struggle continued for thirty years. When that struggle could not turn the Sinhala government's attention towards justice and fairness, an armed struggle continued for twenty-five years. Five hundred thousand Tamil people lost their lives in it.

Even after a democratic election was held in Sri Lanka's Northern Province in September this year and the Tamil National Alliance won thirty of thirty-nine seats and formed the government, the rights justly due to Tamils remain diluted.

I have already cited many times the details now given to a Tamil daily by Sri Lankan Member of Parliament and Eelam People's Revolutionary Liberation Front General Secretary Suresh Premachandran: the human-rights violations unleashed by the Rajapaksa government during the Eelam war, the war crimes committed by trampling international law underfoot, and the planned destruction of the Tamil people continuing after the war. We have also pointed to them through TESO resolutions.

Since the final phase of the Eelam war ended in 2009, the Sri Lankan government's policy of Sinhalisation has been implemented with great intensity. Of its 200,000 military personnel, about 150,000 are stationed in the Tamil-inhabited Northern and Eastern Provinces. Under the pretext of preserving peace and removing landmines, every Tamil area has been militarised.

In the Northern Province, where one million Tamils live, the Sinhala military has taken possession of 6,500 acres belonging to Tamils. Sinhala soldiers continue to occupy and use the homes of Tamil people who fled during the war and were later confined in barbed-wire camps.

Of the 84,000 people widowed by the war, 54,000 live in the Northern Province alone. Twelve thousand orphaned children and 2,000 people with disabilities remain helpless, without support or means of livelihood.

In the Tamil-majority Northern and Eastern Provinces, the Tamil population, stated in the source as having been 7 per cent, has now fallen to 48 per cent. One million Tamils have left Sri Lanka and settled in various countries as members of the diaspora. Only about 1.8 million Tamils now live in these two provinces. Attempts continue to remove even them and complete the Sinhalisation of the region.
Through population reduction and changes to boundaries, even the geography of areas in which Tamils have lived from ancient times is losing its original identity.
Hindu temples and Islamic mosques in Tamil areas are being demolished and turned into Buddhist places of worship.

Those who oppose such authoritarian, fascist actions by the Sinhala government are accused of terrorism. Under the Prevention of Terrorism Act, they may be arrested and imprisoned for one and a half years without trial. Because of these threats, Tamils try to leave their own country and seek a livelihood elsewhere. In another great cruelty, the Sri Lankan government is said to be forcing contraception upon Tamil girls under eighteen, mercilessly obstructing the growth of the community.

After the approach of seeking peace through talks has disappeared and such grave conditions and tensions have developed in Sri Lanka, it is difficult to see what useful result can come from people like Salman seeking to discuss these matters.

Is this the first discussion ever held between India and Sri Lanka? Since the war, countless rounds of talks have taken place in Delhi and Colombo, among officials, among ministers and between Prime Ministers. What resulted? Have human-rights violations declined? Has the military occupying Tamil areas withdrawn? Have Tamil homes and lands under military occupation been returned to their owners? Have Tamils received greater powers?

Rajapaksa promised “Thirteen Plus,” powers beyond even the Thirteenth Amendment. Did he fulfil it? A provincial government elected by the Tamil majority is functioning there without powers over land or the police. Has Rajapaksa given even the slightest thought to that? Is it not his wish that the Tamil government, deprived of governing authority and caught within the Sinhala government's tight control, should become unable to serve the people properly, decline, and become an object of Tamil resentment?

The attacks and damage inflicted upon Tamil Nadu fishers by Sinhala personnel and the Sri Lankan Coast Guard, shootings, prosecutions and imprisonment are increasing day by day after the war, even beyond their pre-war level. They have not decreased in the slightest.

Salman Khurshid says India is involved in various welfare projects for Tamils in the Northern Province. Most of the benefits of those projects go to Sinhalese people. Reports repeatedly say a rule has been framed under which homes are allotted only to families of at least four people, and most newly built houses are consequently being allocated to Sinhalese families.

In April 2011, the three-member panel headed by Indonesia's Marzuki Darusman and appointed by UN Secretary-General Ban Ki-moon reported on attempts by the Rajapaksa government to destroy evidence so its alleged war crimes would not become known outside Sri Lanka. Did the Sri Lankan government awaken even after that report appeared?

It was urged that an international panel be established through the UN Human Rights Council to identify war crimes committed in Sri Lanka and punish those responsible. Did the Sri Lankan government act according to its conscience even after that?

The Sri Lankan government itself appointed the Lessons Learnt and Reconciliation Commission, which submitted a report containing many recommendations. Has the Rajapaksa government ever considered accepting and implementing them?
After touring Sri Lanka for a week last September, UN High Commissioner for Human Rights Navanethem Pillay told reporters openly before the international community:

> Harassment of human-rights activists by Sri Lankan security forces has increased. There are growing signs that Sri Lanka is moving towards authoritarian rule. Women in war-affected areas are unsafe. The Sri Lankan government's commission on missing persons is disappointing.
Did the Sri Lankan government try to reform itself even after that?

Two months ago, Ranil Wickremesinghe, leader of Sri Lanka's opposition United National Party, charged: “Sinhala military settlements are being established everywhere in Jaffna. Within another five years, there may not even be space for Tamil people to live there.” Did the Sri Lankan government take any action to remedy its failings even after that accusation?

In a statement two days ago, British Prime Minister David Cameron pointed out: “Four years after the civil war ended in Sri Lanka, no one has been arrested over allegations of war crimes and sexual violence. Journalists continue to be threatened.” Should the Sinhala government not have bowed its head in shame?

The Canadian Prime Minister announced that he would not attend the Commonwealth meeting in protest against extrajudicial killings and attacks upon minorities in Sri Lanka. Mauritius Prime Minister Navinchandra Ramgoolam, having closely observed the human-rights situation, likewise announced that he would not attend in order to condemn it. Should these decisions not have distressed the Sinhala government and caused it to reflect?
South African peace campaigner and Nobel laureate Archbishop Desmond Tutu said:

> India and all other member countries should boycott the Commonwealth meeting in Sri Lanka. Through this, justice can be secured for the Tamils living there as a minority and for the inhuman acts committed against them.
Did the Sinhala government give even a little thought to his words?

The nations of the world, the international community and global bodies such as the United Nations must find a way to open the door of the Sinhala government's conscience and make it recognise justice and fairness.

There must be an independent and credible investigation into the genocide of Tamils, human-rights violations, war crimes and violations of international law committed in Sri Lanka, and those responsible must be punished. A referendum must be held among Eelam Tamils under UN supervision so they may choose the political settlement they desire. These are the proper answers to the problems imposed upon Eelam Tamils over so many years.
These are the demands advanced by the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam and by TESO.

For now, however, will Indian External Affairs Minister Salman Khurshid, who is in Sri Lanka, hold the talks he himself described and secure concrete arrangements for the complete withdrawal of the military from Tamil areas; all rights due to Tamils under the Thirteenth Amendment; financial, police, land and related powers for the new Northern Provincial government; and an end to the problems faced by Tamil Nadu fishers?

Will he also come forward at the Commonwealth meeting to raise his voice against the Sinhala government's human-rights violations, alleged war crimes and sexual atrocities committed by military personnel? This is the question arising among the Tamil people.
TESO meets tomorrow, 17 November 2013, precisely to discuss these issues and decide what must be done next.
With affection,
M. K.
16 November 2013
